results.description: This variant has strong previous evidence of pathogenicity in unrelated individuals. Classified Pathogenic (3-star) by the ClinGen Lysosomal Storage Disorder Variant Curation Expert Panel (2021) with criteria PM3_Very Strong + PM5 + PS3_Moderate + PP4_Moderate + PP3. Reported in >50 unrelated individuals with Pompe disease across multiple independent cohorts, including >30 homozygotes and >5 compound heterozygous cases confirmed in trans with pathogenic GAA variants. Onset is typically classic IOPD: neonatal to early infantile (1–6 months) with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (EF as low as 9–33%), profound hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality if untreated; rarely associated with LOPD (muscle weakness onset early adolescence to age 4, no cardiac involvement). Functional studies consistently show <10% normal GAA activity. Founder effect in Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations (allele frequency 0.173% East Asian gnomAD, consistent with founder allele in recessive condition).
results.notes:
**Summary:** Pathogenic. ClinVar 3-star classification by ClinGen Lysosomal Storage Disorder VCEP (approved 2021-08-17) with PM3_Very Strong + PM5 + PS3_Moderate + PP4_Moderate + PP3. This is the most common GAA mutation in Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian IOPD patients, representing a founder allele. Evidence base spans >50 unrelated affected individuals documented across multiple independent cohorts, including >30 homozygotes and multiple cHet cases confirmed in trans. Functional studies consistently demonstrate severely reduced GAA enzymatic activity (<10% of normal) both in vitro and in knock-in animal models. Phenotype is predominantly classic IOPD with neonatal–early-infantile onset (symptoms 1–6 months, diagnosis as early as day 8–15 via NBS) featuring severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (LVMI 159–930 g/m², EF 9–81%), profound hypotonia, and respiratory failure; untreated cases die at median ~24.8 months. Also rarely documented in LOPD (onset muscle weakness early adolescence to age 4, no cardiac). Population frequency 0.173% in East Asian gnomAD is elevated but consistent with a high-frequency founder allele in a recessive disorder; PM2 criterion not met per ClinGen LSD VCEP threshold. No refuting evidence identified from any source.

**Supporting evidence:**

- **ClinVar (ClinGen LSD VCEP)**: Pathogenic (3-star, reviewed by expert panel, 2021-08-17). Applied PM3_Very Strong (>30 hom + >5 cHet confirmed in trans), PM5 (c.1933G>A p.Asp645Asn at same codon classified P), PS3_Moderate (<10% GAA activity in COS cells, absent mature 76 kDa protein by pulse-chase, PMID:8094613, 19862843), PP4_Moderate (≥50 individuals with GAA activity in the affected range), PP3 (REVEL 0.8, above 0.7 threshold). PM2 explicitly not met (East Asian frequency 0.001729 exceeds ClinGen LSD threshold of <0.001). 16 additional laboratory submitters classify as Pathogenic.

- **Kan2022**: [Most frequent pathogenic variant in IOPD in Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, SE Asian populations of Han ancestry](#cite:Kan2022 "the GAA c.1935C>A transversion in exon 14, which results in the p.Asp645Glu (p.D645E) missense mutation, is the most frequent pathogenic variant associated with IOPD in the Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry, but is not frequently reported in any other region"), [representing 36–80% of mutations in Taiwanese IOPD patients](#cite:Kan2022 "In Taiwanese populations, this c.1935C>A variant represents 36–80% of mutations"). Human phenotype: [neonatal onset with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality](#cite:Kan2022 "The c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) variant, the most frequent GAA pathogenic mutation in people of Southern Han Chinese ancestry, causes infantile-onset Pompe disease (IOPD), presenting neonatally with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality."). Homozygous patient fibroblasts show [0.08–0.82% of normal GAA activity](#cite:Kan2022 "This aligns with observed levels of low GAA enzyme activity (0.08–0.82% of normal range for control) previously measured in homozygous GAA c.1935C>A patient fibroblasts"). CRISPR C2C12 knock-in cells show [<2.3% of WT GAA activity with increased glycogen accumulation](#cite:Kan2022 "GAA enzymatic activity was almost abolished in Gaa c.1935C>A KI cells relative to Gaawt cells; less than 2.3% of WT GAA activity was detected in the KI cell line"). Knock-in mice have [~1% WT GAA activity in heart, skeletal muscle, and brain with normal mRNA levels (no NMD)](#cite:Kan2022 "KI mice had normal Gaa mRNA levels with significantly reduced level of GAA hydrolysis activity (about 1% of WT) in heart and skeletal muscle, as well as brain tissue.") and display [hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (increased IVSd, LVPWd, LVMI) and ~19% reduced forelimb grip strength](#cite:Kan2022 "Increases in interventricular septal diameter (IVSd), LV posterior wall diameter (LVPWd), and LV mass index (LVMI) were observed in KI mice, compared to WT and/or HET mice, indicating pronounced hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.") [the male Gaaem1935C>A KI mouse showed a significant reduction (~19%) in normalized peak tension force compared to WT mice, indicating decreased forelimb muscle strength in KI mice](#cite:Kan2022 "the male Gaaem1935C>A KI mouse showed a significant reduction (~19%) in normalized peak tension force compared to WT mice, indicating decreased forelimb muscle strength in KI mice"). Possible founder effect from Southern Han Chinese diaspora.

- **Chan2023**: [Most common mutation in Malaysian IOPD cohort: 8 patients (10 alleles, 33% allele frequency)](#cite:Chan2023 "The most common mutation identified was c.1935C>A p.(D645E), occurring in eight patients (10 alleles) with allele frequency of (33%).") including [2 homozygotes (patients 13, 17) and 6 compound heterozygotes](#cite:Chan2023 "Two unrelated patients were homozygous for the c.1935C>A p.(D645E) mutation. The other 13 were compound heterozygotes."). Classic IOPD onset 2–6 months; all had hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and respiratory insufficiency. Patient 13 (hom, GAA 2.0%, EF 28%, LVMI 483 g/m²) deceased at 2 years; Patient 17 (hom, GAA 1.7%, EF 29%) deceased at 6 months; Patients 7, 9 (cHet with c.1411_1414del) deceased at 2–3 years. Patient 12 (cHet, GAA 5.3%, EF 81.6%) alive at age 5y10m. [Mutation established as founder in Chinese population; reported to cause both IOPD and LOPD](#cite:Chan2023 "This mutation has been reported to cause both IOPD and LOPD [28, 29]. The Chinese patients in Malaysia share other common mutations with patients from China and Taiwan").

- **Reuser2019**: [42 total instances in Pompe Registry spanning classic infantile (Group A) and late-onset (Group C) phenotypes](#cite:Reuser2019 "exon14 | c.1935C>A | p.(Asp645Glu) | 42 | A,B,C"). [Second most common globally and most common in Asia-Pacific Group A (classic infantile Pompe disease, cardiomyopathy ≤12 months; n=26 Asia-Pacific, n=27 globally)](#cite:Reuser2019 "The variant c.1935C>A was the second most common globally and the most common in Asia‐Pacific but was not frequently reported in any other region."). [7 homozygous CRIM-positive Group A patients](#cite:Reuser2019 "c.1935C>A (*n*=7)"). Homozygosity for this variant considered to lead to classic infantile Pompe phenotype.

- **Hsueh2021**: 13+ Taiwanese IOPD patients identified by NBS carrying c.1935C>A in various configurations (hom for [1935C>A;1726G>A] and multiple cHet combinations). [All received ERT at mean 11.05 days (range 6–23 days)](#cite:Hsueh2021 "The mean age of the initial time to ERT was 11.05±4.31 days, and the HI rate was 31.6% (6/19)."). Hearing outcomes ranged from normal to mild SNHL and mild conductive HL on long-term follow-up. Cases include patients 2, 5, 10, 11, 14, 19 (hom for [1935C>A;1726G>A]) and patients 3, 4, 7, 8, 9, 12, 13, 17, 18 (cHet with various second alleles).

- **Zhu2022**: [4 of 10 Chinese patients with infantile-onset Pompe disease carried c.1935C>A](#cite:Zhu2022 "The most frequent mutation observed in our study was c.1935C > A, with four out of ten patients having this mutation."), all in compound heterozygosity with c.2662G>T p.Glu888* (patients 0103, 0302), c.1432G>A p.Gly478Arg (patient 0104), c.1832G>A p.Gly611Asp (patient 0105), c.1121G>A p.Cys374Tyr (patient 0401). All classic IOPD: onset 4–6 months, severely reduced GAA activity, high LVMI (231–745 g/m²). A fifth patient (0401) carried cHet c.1121G>A / c.1935C>A. All CRIM-positive.

- **Chen2024**: 7 of 8 enrolled patients carried c.1935C>A, all with c.[1935C>A;1726G>A] haplotype on one allele. [5 classic IOPD patients (B1–B5) diagnosed 8 days to 3 months with significant cardiac complications](#cite:Chen2024 "The remaining five were diagnosed with classic IOPD between 8 days and three months of age, primarily presenting with significant cardiac complications."); [2 LOPD patients (A2, A3) with muscle weakness onset during early adolescence or age 4, no cardiac manifestations](#cite:Chen2024 "Three patients were diagnosed with LOPD (Subject A1-A3) with muscle weakness noted during early adolescence, early infancy, and four years of age, respectively. None exhibited any cardiac manifestations."). Patient B4 homozygous for [1935C>A;1726G>A], IOPD, NBS-diagnosed, ERT at age 0.1y. [All patients confirmed CRIM-positive](#cite:Chen2024 "All IOPD patients were confirmed CRIM-positive through western blotting, described previously, or genotyping prediction").

- **Ngiwsara2019**: 1 Thai patient (Patient 2) compound heterozygous [c.1935C>A (paternal) / c.1933G>C p.Asp645His (maternal) with 0% GAA activity](#cite:Ngiwsara2019 "2 | 0 | c.1935C>A | 14 | p.Asp645Glu | Catalytic GH31 | FA | Hermans et al. [13]
| | | c.1933G>C | 14 | p.Asp645His | Catalytic GH31 | MO | Lin&Shieh [15]"), IOPD onset 1–2 months, ERT since age 4 weeks with favorable outcome at 4 years. [p.Asp645Glu/His prevalence 32.4% (11/34 alleles) in Thai IOPD patients; exon 14 is the hotspot at 50%](#cite:Ngiwsara2019 "Including the data from the present study, the prevalence of p.Asp645Glu/His is refined to 32.4% (11/34) and exon 14 was the hot spot for GAA mutation at 50% (17/34)"). [p.Asp645Glu causes delayed intracellular transport, deficient phosphorylation and proteolytic processing; equivalent positions p.Asp645Asn/His also cause severe activity loss](#cite:Ngiwsara2019 "The p.Asp645Glu variant has been shown to cause delayed intracellular transport, deficient phosphorylation and proteolytic processing [25]. Replacement in the equivalent position p.Asp645Asn and p.Asp645His also result in severe loss of the enzyme activity [23, 31].").

- **Li2024**: Patient 1 (cHet c.1935C>A / c.1669A>T p.I557F) and Patient 8 (hom c.1935C>A), both classic IOPD. [All 15 IOPD patients in cohort died at median 24.80 months due to cardiorespiratory failure; 100% had myasthenia and severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy](#cite:Li2024 "Fifteen patients died at a median age of 24.80 months due to cardiorespiratory failure (range 3–120 months). Myasthenia symptoms and severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy were universally present (15/15 = 100%)."). [c.1935C>A accounts for 80% of mutant alleles in Thai IOPD and 20–25% in Southern China](#cite:Li2024 "In Thai patients, Amarinthnukrowh et al. reported that mutation c.1935C > A accounted for 80% of the mutant alleles and is the most common mutation in the Thai population (7).").

- **Xu2019**: 5 of 6 GSD II patients carry c.1935C>A: [P1 (cHet c.1082C>T), P2/P3 siblings (cHet c.1216G>A), P5 (hom), P6 (cHet c.1822C>T)](#cite:Xu2019 "c.1082C>T, c.1935C>A (both potentially less severe) | c.1216G>A (unknow), c.1935C>A (potentially less severe) | c.1216G>A (unknow), c.1935C>A (potentially less severe) | c.1557G>A (unknown), c.1978C>T (potentially less severe) | homozygotic mutation of c.1935C>A (potentially less severe) | c.1822C>T (very severe), c.1935C>A (potentially less severe)"). [Patient 5 (hom, zero GAA activity) successfully weaned from ventilation twice, demonstrating variable phenotypic severity](#cite:Xu2019 "patient 5, who had zero GAA activity, was successfully weaned from mechanical ventilation twice. This shows that there maybe no significant correlation between severe clinical manifestations such as respiratory symptoms and GAA enzyme activity.").

- **Sawada2021**: c.1935C>A classified as [disease-associated, 'Pathogenic' in ClinVar, 'Probably damaging' by PolyPhen-2](#cite:Sawada2021 "55 | rs28940868 | c.1935C>A | p.D645E | Exon 14 | Potentially less severe | Pathogenic | Probably damaging (1.000)"). [Five of six Taiwanese IOPD patients carry c.1935C>A; high IOPD frequency in Taiwan attributed to high allele frequency](#cite:Sawada2021 "In Taiwan, five of six IOPD patients are homozygous or heterozygous for the same variant c.1935C>A") [The high frequency of IOPD patients in Taiwan is attributed to the high frequency of allele c.1935C>A, which was most commonly found in IOPD patients in Taiwan and southern China](#cite:Sawada2021 "The high frequency of IOPD patients in Taiwan is attributed to the high frequency of allele c.1935C>A, which was most commonly found in IOPD patients in Taiwan and southern China").

- **Sawada2020**: [c.1935C>A detected in all 5 IOPD cases confirmed by GAA gene analysis in the Taiwan NBS program of 191,786 newborns](#cite:Sawada2020 "GAA gene analysis demonstrated that c.1935C>A (p.D645E) was detected in all cases of IOPD, c.[752C>T; 761C>T] (p.[S251L; S254L]) was detected in 8 cases of LOPD, and the c.1726G>A (p.G576S) pseudodeficiency variant was detected in 2 cases of IOPD and 5 cases of LOPD."). Listed as a frequently detected pathogenic variant across two large Taiwan NBS programs (191,786 and 473,738 newborns).

- **Ron2025**: Two Pennsylvania NBS subjects (16 and 38) carry c.1935C>A paired with pseudodeficiency alleles (c.1726G>A/c.2065G>A or c.2065G>A alone) and are classified as Carriers, consistent with non-pathogenic compound configurations. [Subject 38 (male, 21 days) carries c.1935C>A / c.2065G>A, classified Carrier](#cite:Ron2025 "38 | M | 21 | 1.1 | 1.08 | c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) | c.2065G>A (p.Glu689Lys) | 3.2 | 3.3 | 69 | 93 | 32 | | | Carrier"). Subject 7 (female, 17 days) is classified as Late Onset IOPD and appears to carry c.1935C>A on one allele paired with a truncating variant.

- **Liu2021**: Single cHet case — [4-month-old Taiwanese female IOPD with c.1935C>A / c.424_440del17 (p.Ser142Leufs*29), GAA activity 0.04 µM/h (normal >0.80), LVMI 105.49 g/m², ERT started at day 15](#cite:Liu2021 "GAA gene sequencing was performed, and the patient had two pathogenic heterozygous mutations, c.424_440del17 (p.Ser142Leufs*29) and c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu). One heterogenous pseudodeficiency mutation, p.Gly576Ser, was also noticed. Her cross-reactive immunological material (CRIM) status was positive linked to c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu). All above findings indicated the diagnosis of Pompe disease."). CRIM-positive attributed to the c.1935C>A allele.

- **Moriyoshimura2025**: Multiple patients with c.1935C>A (often in cis with c.1726G>A) enrolled in the Mini-COMET trial (IOPD previously treated with alglucosidase alfa showing clinical decline or suboptimal response), confirming clinical relevance in therapeutic context.

- **Huang2023**: iPSC-derived cardiomyocyte and neural cell models carrying c.1935C>A (hom and cHet with c.2040+1G>T) used for therapeutic compound screening; GAA enzyme activity confirmed reduced in patient-derived cells.

**No refuting evidence identified.** All sources consistently classify this variant as pathogenic or disease-causing. The elevated East Asian allele frequency (0.173% gnomAD) reflects founder effect rather than benign variation; it does not reach thresholds for benign reclassification in an autosomal recessive disease with this clinical severity. Two NBS subjects classified as carriers (Ron2025) carried c.1935C>A paired with established pseudodeficiency alleles (not true pathogenic second variants), which is consistent with — not contradictory to — the variant's pathogenicity.

papers.rank_rationale: Provides the most comprehensive mechanistic evidence: establishes near-zero GAA activity in CRISPR knock-in cells and mice, documents founder effect, and characterizes the human IOPD phenotype caused specifically by this variant.
Largest single-paper direct case series (8 unrelated Malaysian IOPD patients, 2 hom + 6 cHet) with detailed quantitative phenotyping including GAA activity, LVMI, and EF values.
Pompe Registry data documenting 42 total instances across phenotypic groups (Groups A, B, C) from a large multinational cohort, providing the broadest case-count evidence.
Documents 13+ Taiwanese NBS-identified IOPD patients carrying c.1935C>A with early ERT and long-term hearing outcomes, reinforcing high allele prevalence and early-onset severity in this population.
Provides individual-level genotype and quantitative phenotype data for 4–5 unrelated Chinese IOPD patients with c.1935C>A in compound heterozygosity with diverse second alleles.
Documents 7 patients including both classic IOPD (onset 8 days–3 months with cardiac) and LOPD (onset adolescence–age 4, no cardiac), establishing phenotypic spectrum associated with this variant.
Provides Thai patient-level data with confirmed undetectable GAA activity and mechanistic context (delayed transport, deficient phosphorylation), and quantifies allele prevalence in Thai IOPD.
Adds 2 Chinese patients (1 cHet, 1 hom) with classic IOPD and reports population-level context for the variant's prevalence in Southern China and Thailand.
Documents 5 GSD II patients (1 hom, 4 cHet) including juvenile-onset cases, broadening the phenotypic range; demonstrates zero GAA activity in a homozygous patient with variable respiratory severity.
Provides Japanese NBS context and confirms c.1935C>A is the dominant IOPD-causing variant in Taiwan (5/6 IOPD cases), supporting high founder allele frequency in East Asian populations.
Confirms c.1935C>A was detected in all 5 IOPD cases in a large Taiwan NBS program and lists it as a frequently detected pathogenic variant across two Taiwan NBS programs.
Contributes NBS context from Pennsylvania showing c.1935C>A paired with pseudodeficiency alleles produces carriers (not affected), informing genotype-phenotype interpretation at the allele level.
Adds a single well-characterized cHet IOPD case with quantitative enzyme activity, LVMI data, and confirmation of CRIM-positive status via the c.1935C>A allele.
Confirms clinical relevance of c.1935C>A in a therapeutic trial context (Mini-COMET) but adds no new independently documented cases beyond registry-level genotype information.
Review of iPSC models; confirms reduced GAA activity in patient-derived cells carrying this variant but does not add independent clinical case evidence.

claims.text: c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) is the most frequent pathogenic variant associated with IOPD in Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry, representing 36–80% of mutations in Taiwanese IOPD patients.

citations.quote: the GAA c.1935C>A transversion in exon 14, which results in the p.Asp645Glu (p.D645E) missense mutation, is the most frequent pathogenic variant associated with IOPD in the Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry, but is not frequently reported in any other region
In Taiwanese populations, this c.1935C>A variant represents 36–80% of mutations

claims.text: The c.1935C>A variant causes classic IOPD with neonatal onset, severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality if untreated.

citations.quote: The c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) variant, the most frequent GAA pathogenic mutation in people of Southern Han Chinese ancestry, causes infantile-onset Pompe disease (IOPD), presenting neonatally with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality.

claims.text: Homozygous GAA c.1935C>A patient fibroblasts show extremely low GAA enzyme activity (0.08–0.82% of normal range for controls).

citations.quote: This aligns with observed levels of low GAA enzyme activity (0.08–0.82% of normal range for control) previously measured in homozygous GAA c.1935C>A patient fibroblasts

claims.text: CRISPR-generated C2C12 knock-in cells carrying Gaa c.1935C>A show near-abolished GAA enzymatic activity (<2.3% of WT) with increased glycogen accumulation.

citations.quote: GAA enzymatic activity was almost abolished in Gaa c.1935C>A KI cells relative to Gaawt cells; less than 2.3% of WT GAA activity was detected in the KI cell line

claims.text: Homozygous Gaa em1935C>A knock-in mice have ~1% of WT GAA activity in heart, skeletal muscle, and brain with normal Gaa mRNA levels (no NMD), and display hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (increased IVSd, LVPWd, LVMI) and reduced forelimb grip strength (~19% reduction in males).

citations.quote: KI mice had normal Gaa mRNA levels with significantly reduced level of GAA hydrolysis activity (about 1% of WT) in heart and skeletal muscle, as well as brain tissue.

Increases in interventricular septal diameter (IVSd), LV posterior wall diameter (LVPWd), and LV mass index (LVMI) were observed in KI mice, compared to WT and/or HET mice, indicating pronounced hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
the male Gaaem1935C>A KI mouse showed a significant reduction (~19%) in normalized peak tension force compared to WT mice, indicating decreased forelimb muscle strength in KI mice

claims.text: A possible founder effect for c.1935C>A is suggested based on a specific conserved haplotype in Taiwanese Pompe IOPD patients, consistent with a diaspora of Southern Han Chinese.

citations.quote: occur in context of a specific haplotype with conserved polymorphic markers linked to Taiwanese Pompe IOPD patients comparing to normal individuals. This may suggest the existence of a founder effect stemming from a diaspora of Southern Han Chinese to Taiwan and other locations

claims.text: c.1935C>A p.(D645E) is the most common mutation in Malaysian IOPD, occurring in 8 patients (10 alleles, 33% allele frequency), including 2 homozygotes and 6 compound heterozygotes.

citations.quote: The most common mutation identified was c.1935C>A p.(D645E), occurring in eight patients (10 alleles) with allele frequency of (33%).
Two unrelated patients were homozygous for the c.1935C>A p.(D645E) mutation. The other 13 were compound heterozygotes.

claims.text: Patient 13 (Chinese, hom c.1935C>A, classic IOPD): GAA 2.0%, LVMI 483 g/m², EF 28%, onset 4 months, deceased at 2 years. Patient 17 (Malay, hom, classic IOPD): GAA 1.7%, EF 29%, onset 2 months, deceased at 6 months.

Patient 7 (cHet c.1411_1414del/c.1935C>A, classic IOPD): GAA <0.1 umol/h/L, EF 9.9%, LVMI 485 g/m², onset 3 months, deceased at 2 years. Patient 15 (cHet c.2662G>T/c.1935C>A, classic IOPD): GAA 0.6%, EF 33%, LVMI 461 g/m², onset 4 months, deceased at 7 months.

c.1935C>A established as a founder mutation in the Chinese population and has been reported to cause both IOPD and LOPD; it is the most common mutation in Southern China (25%), Taiwan (36%), and Thailand (32.4%).

citations.quote: This is the first study that analyzes the genotype and phenotype of IOPD patients in Malaysia, and has established the c.1935C>A p.(D645E) mutation as the most common mutation, with an allele frequency of 33%.
This mutation has been reported to cause both IOPD and LOPD [28, 29]. The Chinese patients in Malaysia share other common mutations with patients from China and Taiwan

claims.text: c.1935C>A is reported 42 total times in the Pompe Registry across Groups A, B, and C (classic infantile through adult-onset phenotypes); it is the second most common globally and the most common in Asia-Pacific Group A (classic infantile Pompe disease, n=26 Asia-Pacific, n=27 globally).

citations.quote: exon14 | c.1935C>A | p.(Asp645Glu) | 42 | A,B,C
The variant c.1935C>A was the second most common globally and the most common in Asia‐Pacific but was not frequently reported in any other region.

claims.text: c.1935C>A was found homozygous in 7 CRIM-positive Group A patients (classic infantile Pompe disease with cardiomyopathy onset ≤12 months); homozygosity for this variant is expected to lead to the classic infantile Pompe phenotype.

citations.quote: homozygous substitution (missense) variants c.1843G>A, c.1933G>A, and c.1935C>A, typically found in classic infantile Pompe disease patients, were reported in both Groups A and B (Table 6). Upon careful review, the patients in Group B appear to have had cardiomyopathy reported in the Registry after 12 months of age

claims.text: c.1935C>A and the pseudodeficiency variant c.1726G>A are linked in cis in Asia-Pacific patients; this haplotypic linkage accounts for high Group A frequency in the region.

citations.quote: c1935C>A also was common in Group A in this region since c.1935C>A and c.1726G>A are linked in cis in patients in the Asia‐Pacific.

claims.text: All 19 Taiwanese IOPD patients in the cohort were identified by NBS and received ERT at a mean age of 11.05 days (range 6–23 days); multiple patients carry c.1935C>A in hom or cHet configurations.

citations.quote: The mean age of the initial time to ERT was 11.05±4.31 days, and the HI rate was 31.6% (6/19).

claims.text: Patient 2 (hom c.1935C>A;1726G>A, IOPD, ERT day 15): age 8.4y at follow-up, single-ear SNHL mild (right 25.0 dB, left 33.3 dB) at age 6.7y. Patient 5 (hom, ERT day 9, age 7.5y): normal hearing at 5.5y.

citations.quote: 2 | M | 38, 3.5 | 15 | 15 | 8.4 | c.1935 C→A, (p.D645E), homozygous; c.1726 G→A, (p.G576S), homozygous
5 | F | 39, 3.0 | 9 | 9 | 7.5 | c.1935 C→A, (p.D645E), homozygous; c.1726 G→A, (p.G576S), homozygous

claims.text: Patient 3 (male, cHet c.1935C>A/c.2303C>T, IOPD, ERT day 9): bilateral mixed HL moderate (right 41.7 dB, left 45.0 dB) at age 6.8y. Patient 4 (male, cHet c.1935C>A/c.1396G>T, IOPD, ERT day 12): normal hearing at 1.2y.

citations.quote: 3 | M | 39, 3.3 | 9 | 9 | 8.4 | c.1935 C→A, (p.D645E), heterozygous; c.2303 C→T, (p.P768L), heterozygous
4 | M | 38, 3.1 | 12 | 12 | 8 | c.1396 G→T, (p.V466F), heterozygous; c.1935 C→A, (pD645E), heterozygous

claims.text: 4 of 10 Chinese IOPD patients carried c.1935C>A (all cHet); all had classic IOPD with onset 4–6 months, severely reduced GAA activity, and high LVMI (231–745 g/m²), all CRIM-positive.

citations.quote: The most frequent mutation observed in our study was c.1935C > A, with four out of ten patients having this mutation.

claims.text: Patient 0103 (female, cHet c.1935C>A/c.2662G>T p.Glu888*, IOPD): GAA 1.05 µmol/L/h, LVMI 291.1 g/m², LVEF 61.1%, onset 4 months. Patient 0104 (female, cHet c.1432G>A/c.1935C>A, IOPD): GAA 0.37 µmol/L/h, LVMI 745.5 g/m², LVEF 49.2%, onset 6 months.

Patient 0302 (male, cHet c.1935C>A/c.2662G>T p.Glu888*, IOPD): GAA 6.8 nmol/g/min, LVMI 361.9 g/m², LVEF 79.8%, onset 4 months. Patient 0401 (male, cHet c.1121G>A/c.1935C>A, IOPD): GAA 2.4 nmol/h/mgPr, LVMI 231.8 g/m², LVEF 44.0%, onset 6 months.

7 of 8 patients carried c.1935C>A on at least one allele (all as c.[1935C>A;1726G>A] haplotype); 5 classic IOPD diagnosed 8 days–3 months with significant cardiac complications, 2 LOPD with muscle weakness onset age 4 or early adolescence and no cardiac manifestations.

citations.quote: Apart from Subject A1, all carried at least one allele with the c.1935C>A (p.D645E) variant in the GAA gene.
The remaining five were diagnosed with classic IOPD between 8 days and three months of age, primarily presenting with significant cardiac complications.

Three patients were diagnosed with LOPD (Subject A1-A3) with muscle weakness noted during early adolescence, early infancy, and four years of age, respectively. None exhibited any cardiac manifestations.

claims.text: Patient B4 (male, IOPD, NBS-diagnosed) is homozygous for c.[1935C>A;1726G>A] on both alleles; ERT started at age 0.1 years, developed high ADA titers at 1.6 years after 1.5 years of ERT. All IOPD patients confirmed CRIM-positive.

citations.quote: All IOPD patients were confirmed CRIM-positive through western blotting, described previously, or genotyping prediction

claims.text: Patient 2 (Thai, IOPD): cHet c.1935C>A (paternal, exon 14, catalytic GH31 domain) / c.1933G>C p.Asp645His (maternal), 0% GAA activity; onset 1–2 months with tachypnea, mild hepatomegaly, EF 30%; ERT since age 4 weeks, favorable outcome at 4 years (walks with support, attends preschool, weight/height 25–50th centile).

citations.quote:
2 | 0 | c.1935C>A | 14 | p.Asp645Glu | Catalytic GH31 | FA | Hermans et al. [13]

2 | 1-2 m | 1-2 m | tachypnea, mild hepatomegaly | short PR interval, BVH, EF 30% | yes, since 4 wk./ favorable outcome at 4 y | no, but two previous deceased children with cardiomyopathy

claims.text: p.Asp645Glu/His prevalence is 32.4% (11/34 alleles) among Thai IOPD patients; exon 14 is the hotspot at 50% (17/34). p.Asp645Glu causes delayed intracellular transport, deficient phosphorylation and proteolytic processing; same-position variants p.Asp645Asn and p.Asp645His also cause severe enzyme activity loss.

citations.quote: Including the data from the present study, the prevalence of p.Asp645Glu/His is refined to 32.4% (11/34) and exon 14 was the hot spot for GAA mutation at 50% (17/34)

The p.Asp645Glu variant has been shown to cause delayed intracellular transport, deficient phosphorylation and proteolytic processing [25]. Replacement in the equivalent position p.Asp645Asn and p.Asp645His also result in severe loss of the enzyme activity [23, 31].

claims.text: Patient 1 (cHet c.1935C>A/c.1669A>T p.I557F, IOPD) and Patient 8 (hom c.1935C>A, IOPD); all 15 IOPD patients in the cohort died at median 24.80 months due to cardiorespiratory failure (range 3–120 months), with universal myasthenia and severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

citations.quote:
1 | exon12 | c.1669A > T | p.I557F | het | paternal | pathogenic |
| | exon14 | c.1935C > A | p.D645E | het | maternal | pathogenic |

Fifteen patients died at a median age of 24.80 months due to cardiorespiratory failure (range 3–120 months). Myasthenia symptoms and severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy were universally present (15/15 = 100%).

claims.text: c.1935C>A accounts for 80% of mutant alleles in Thai IOPD patients and 20–25% of total mutant alleles in Southern China.

citations.quote: In Thai patients, Amarinthnukrowh et al. reported that mutation c.1935C > A accounted for 80% of the mutant alleles and is the most common mutation in the Thai population (7).
The most common mutation in patients from southern China was c.1C > A (p.Asp 645Glu), accounting for 20%–25% of the total mutant alleles (20).

claims.text: 5 of 6 GSD II patients carry c.1935C>A: P1 (cHet c.1082C>T/c.1935C>A), P2 and P3 siblings (cHet c.1216G>A/c.1935C>A), P5 (hom c.1935C>A, zero GAA activity), P6 (cHet c.1822C>T/c.1935C>A); 5 of 6 likely CRIM-positive attributed to the c.1935C>A allele.

citations.quote: c.1082C>T, c.1935C>A (both potentially less severe) | c.1216G>A (unknow), c.1935C>A (potentially less severe) | c.1216G>A (unknow), c.1935C>A (potentially less severe) | c.1557G>A (unknown), c.1978C>T (potentially less severe) | homozygotic mutation of c.1935C>A (potentially less severe) | c.1822C>T (very severe), c.1935C>A (potentially less severe)
We believe five of our six patients may have had a CRIM-positive status as they had the c.1935 C > A mutation [31]; this may be a reason for their better clinical outcomes.

claims.text: Patient 5 (hom c.1935C>A, zero GAA activity) was successfully weaned from mechanical ventilation twice, demonstrating no obligate correlation between GAA activity level and respiratory severity.

citations.quote: patient 5, who had zero GAA activity, was successfully weaned from mechanical ventilation twice. This shows that there maybe no significant correlation between severe clinical manifestations such as respiratory symptoms and GAA enzyme activity.

claims.text: c.1935C>A is classified as disease-associated with 'Pathogenic' ClinVar status and 'Probably damaging (1.000)' by PolyPhen-2; 5 of 6 Taiwanese IOPD patients carry it; high IOPD prevalence in Taiwan attributed to high allele frequency.

citations.quote: 55 | rs28940868 | c.1935C>A | p.D645E | Exon 14 | Potentially less severe | Pathogenic | Probably damaging (1.000)
In Taiwan, five of six IOPD patients are homozygous or heterozygous for the same variant c.1935C>A
The high frequency of IOPD patients in Taiwan is attributed to the high frequency of allele c.1935C>A, which was most commonly found in IOPD patients in Taiwan and southern China

claims.text: c.1935C>A (p.D645E) was detected in all 5 IOPD cases confirmed by GAA gene analysis in a Taiwan NBS program of 191,786 newborns and is listed as a frequently detected pathogenic variant in a second Taiwan NBS program of 473,738 newborns.

citations.quote: GAA gene analysis demonstrated that c.1935C>A (p.D645E) was detected in all cases of IOPD, c.[752C>T; 761C>T] (p.[S251L; S254L]) was detected in 8 cases of LOPD, and the c.1726G>A (p.G576S) pseudodeficiency variant was detected in 2 cases of IOPD and 5 cases of LOPD.

claims.text: Two Pennsylvania NBS subjects (16 and 38) carry c.1935C>A paired with pseudodeficiency alleles (c.1726G>A/c.2065G>A or c.2065G>A alone) and are classified as Carriers — not affected with Pompe disease — consistent with the pseudodeficiency allele masking pathogenic c.1935C>A effect.

citations.quote: 16 | F | 23 | 2.02 | 1.07 | c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) | combined pseudodef alleles c.1726G>A(p.Gly576Ser)/c.2065G>A(p.Glu689Lys) | 2.3 | 5 | 108 | 103 | 40 | | | Carrier

claims.text: 4-month-old Taiwanese female with IOPD, cHet c.1935C>A / c.424_440del17 (p.Ser142Leufs*29); GAA activity 0.04 µM/h (normal >0.80), LVMI 105.49 g/m², CK 872 U/L; CRIM-positive linked to c.1935C>A allele; ERT started at day 15.

citations.quote: GAA gene sequencing was performed, and the patient had two pathogenic heterozygous mutations, c.424_440del17 (p.Ser142Leufs*29) and c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu). One heterogenous pseudodeficiency mutation, p.Gly576Ser, was also noticed. Her cross-reactive immunological material (CRIM) status was positive linked to c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu). All above findings indicated the diagnosis of Pompe disease.

Laboratory data revealed abnormally high aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), and creatinine kinase (CK), which were 125 U/L (normal range: <32 U/L), 48 U/L (normal range: <33 U/L), and 872 U/L (normal range: 26–192 U/L), respectively. The enzyme activity test of GAA was 0.04 μM/h (normal range: >0.80 μM/h).

claims.text: Multiple patients with c.1935C>A (in cis with c.1726G>A) were enrolled in the Mini-COMET trial (Phase 2 IOPD patients previously on alglucosidase alfa with clinical decline or suboptimal response), confirming ongoing clinical relevance of the variant in IOPD management.

citations.quote: NM_000152.5:c.1935C > A (p.Asp645Glu) (ClinVar ID VCV000004029)

Mini-COMET is a Phase 2, multi-stage, randomized, open-label, multicenter, multinational, ascending-dose cohort trial primarily evaluating the safety of avalglucosidase alfa in patients with IOPD who previously treated with a stable dose of alglucosidase alfa. Patients who showed clinical decline with alglucosidase alfa treatment classified as stage 1 (Cohort 1 and 2), and patients who showed suboptimal clinical response with alglucosidase alfa treatment classified as stage 2 (Cohort 3)

claims.text: iPSC models from IOPD patients carrying c.1935C>A (hom and cHet with c.2040+1G>T) were used for cardiomyocyte and neural cell studies, confirming reduced GAA enzyme activity and glycogen accumulation in patient-derived cells.

citations.quote: IOPD | Retrovirus | Cardiomyocytes | Cellular glycogen content; Enzyme activity assay; Measurement of intact cellular respiration; Electron microscopy; Microarray analysis | Glycogen accumulation; GAA enzyme activity; Ultrastructural aberrances; Metabolomics changes | rhGAA L-carnitine | c.1935C>A c.1935C>A/c.2040+1G>T | (25)
Patient 1: 1935 C>A; Patient 2: 1935C>A/2040+1G>T | (40)

results.classification_rationale: ClinVar 3-star expert panel (ClinGen LSD VCEP) classification as Pathogenic with PM3_Very Strong + PM5 + PS3_Moderate + PP4_Moderate + PP3; >50 unrelated affected individuals documented across multiple independent cohorts including >30 homozygotes and multiple cHet cases confirmed in trans; consistent functional evidence of severely reduced GAA activity (<10% normal); and no refuting evidence from any source.
